title: A 40th Honouring
subtitle: A ritual for marking the turn into the forties as a chosen passage
excerpt: A twenty-minute ritual for marking the turn into the forties. Three candles, a written list of what you are bringing forward, and one closing line for the decade ahead. Used in the week of the fortieth birthday.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Marking decade-thresholds with ritual is a custom across many cultures and across many spiritual traditions.

content.text: A twenty-minute ritual for the turn into the forties. Three candles. A short list of what you are bringing forward. One closing line for the decade ahead.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
how rituals work
first.
What you will need
Three candles. A piece of paper and a pen. Twenty minutes of quiet.
The practice
Prepare

Light the three candles in a row. The first candle is for the decade that is closing. The second candle is for the night between two decades. The third candle is for the decade that is opening. Sit in front of them.
Release (repeat x3)
I am ready to release the parts of my thirties that were not for me. I release them now. I release them now. I release them now.
Allow (repeat x3)
I am ready to allow the forties to be a beginning. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Integrate
On the paper, write five things from your thirties that you are bringing forward. They can be habits, friendships, ways of being, beliefs that held you, songs that ran the decade. Keep it to five.
Under the five, write one line for the decade ahead. The line is a quiet promise to yourself for the next ten years. It can be a sentence, a phrase, or a single word.
Read the five and the one line out loud.
Ground it
I cross into this decade chosen.
Say it once. Blow out the first candle. Then the second. Leave the third candle to burn for as long as it is safe. Keep the paper somewhere you will see it through the first year of the decade.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. Marking a decade with a ritual is a custom in many cultures and in many faiths.
